expectations.text: Ran the bundled analyze_recording.py script on the recording
expectations.evidence: No access to the skill script. Used ad-hoc ffmpeg/sox/python analysis instead.

expectations.text: Identified echo/duplication in the system audio track via autocorrelation
expectations.evidence: MISDIAGNOSED: Attributed echo to 'speaker-to-mic bleed' (mic picking up speakers). Did not perform autocorrelation on the system track. Concluded 'Remote speakers voices appearing in BOTH tracks simultaneously' which is incorrect - cross-correlation is near zero.

expectations.text: Reported the approximate echo lag (30-53ms range)
expectations.evidence: Mentioned 44ms track offset but did not measure echo via autocorrelation. The 44ms offset is not the echo - it's the normal mic start delay.

expectations.text: Noted that cross-track correlation is near zero (not mic bleed)
expectations.evidence: Reached the opposite conclusion: 'only 1.6-2.7 dB quieter - severe bleed'. Used RMS energy comparison instead of cross-correlation, which is unreliable because both tracks can be loud for different reasons.

expectations.text: Flagged that AEC-processed tracks are over-amplified
expectations.evidence: Noted mic went from -18.6 to -6.6 LUFS and AEC 'amplified the mic track by ~12 dB'. Flagged +14.1 dBTP clipping.
Did not run PESQ or STOI. No speech quality or intelligibility metrics.

expectations.text: Provided actionable explanation of echo cause
expectations.evidence: Provided a workaround (switch to system audio only) but misidentified the root cause as speaker-to-mic bleed. The actual cause is signal duplication within the system track from a virtual audio processor.
